name: TypeScript
description: JavaScript with static types. Catch errors at compile time and write more maintainable large-scale applications.

lessons.title: Types & Type Annotations
lessons.content: TypeScript adds static typing to JavaScript. You annotate variables and function parameters with types using a colon. If you assign the wrong type, the compiler errors before your code runs.

exercises.question: What is the TypeScript type annotation for text values?
Where do you place a type annotation for a variable?
options: At the end of the file

lessons.title: Interfaces & Type Aliases
lessons.content: Interfaces define the shape of objects — what properties and types they must have. Type aliases create reusable type names. Both help document and enforce data structures across your codebase.

exercises.question: What does a ? after a property name mean in an interface?
hints: role?: string — the ? makes it optional

lessons.title: Generics
lessons.content: Generics let you write reusable functions and classes that work with any type while preserving type safety. The type parameter T is a placeholder replaced when the function is called.

exercises.question: What is the purpose of generics in TypeScript?
options: To write reusable code that works with any type safely
exercises.answer: To write reusable code that works with any type safely
hints: Think of T as a type variable — it gets filled in later

lessons.title: Enums
lessons.content: Enums define a set of named constants. Numeric enums auto-increment from 0. String enums give each member an explicit string value. Enums make intent clearer than raw numbers or strings.

exercises.question: What value does the first member of a numeric enum get by default?
hints: Enums start counting from 0 unless you specify otherwise
